q: What does it mean that TypeScript is structurally typed, and how does it differ from nominal typing?
a: TypeScript checks type compatibility by shape, not by name or declared inheritance: if a value has all the required properties with compatible types, it is assignable, regardless of which class or interface it was declared as. Nominal languages like Java or C# require an explicit declared relationship (the same named type or an explicit 'implements'/'extends'). Structural typing is what lets TS describe JavaScript's ad-hoc object literals and duck-typed APIs.
fs: Compatibility is by shape, not by name; that is why two unrelated interfaces with the same members are interchangeable.
fj: Claiming TS works like Java where you must explicitly implement an interface for a value to match it.

lines: const b: B = a // OK: same shape

followups.q: If two structurally identical types are interchangeable, how do you simulate nominal typing when you need a `UserId` and `OrderId` to not be confused?
followups.a: Use branded (opaque) types: intersect the primitive with a unique phantom tag, like `type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: 'UserId' }`. The brand never exists at runtime but makes the two distinct at compile time, so a raw `string` or an `OrderId` is no longer assignable. You construct them through a single asserting factory function.

followups.q: Does structural compatibility apply to `private`/`protected` class members the same way?
followups.a: No, that is the one nominal exception. Two classes with identically-named `private` or `protected` members are NOT compatible unless the member originates from the same declaration. TS tracks the declaring class for private fields, so structurally identical private members from unrelated classes are rejected.

followups.q: How does structural typing interact with function parameter compatibility for object arguments?
followups.a: A function expecting `{ id: number }` accepts any value with at least that shape, so a richer object with extra fields is assignable (extra properties are fine through a variable). Compatibility is one-directional: the supplied type must have everything the parameter needs, not vice versa. This is bivariance/contravariance territory for the parameters themselves.

q: If structural typing lets any same-shaped object through, why does assigning an object literal with an extra property error?
a: Object literals get excess property checking: when you assign a fresh literal directly to a typed target, TS flags properties not present in the target type to catch typos. It is not part of structural assignability; it is a heuristic on literals only. Assigning the literal to a variable first, or widening with a type, bypasses it, which is why a same-shaped value held in a variable is accepted but the inline literal is not.
fs: Excess property checks fire only on fresh object literals; assign through a variable and structural rules take over.
fj: Thinking the extra property is rejected everywhere, or that the check is part of normal assignability.

lines: f(o) // OK: not a fresh literal

followups.q: How do you intentionally allow arbitrary extra properties on a type while still requiring the known ones?
followups.a: Add an index signature like `[key: string]: unknown` (or a more specific value type) to the target type. Excess property checking is suppressed when the target has a compatible index signature, since any extra key is now structurally valid. This is the deliberate escape hatch for genuinely open-ended option bags.

followups.q: Does spreading an object into a literal still trigger excess property checks?
followups.a: No. A spread like `{ ...base, width: 1 }` is not treated as a fresh literal for the spread-in properties, so excess keys coming from `...base` are not flagged. Only the explicitly written literal properties are subject to the check, which is a common way people accidentally pass through extra fields.

followups.q: Why is excess property checking considered a heuristic rather than a soundness guarantee?
followups.a: Because it only catches mistakes at the single point of a fresh literal assignment; the underlying type system still permits same-shaped-plus-extra objects everywhere else. It is a developer-experience feature to catch typos like `colour` vs `color`, not a structural rule, so relying on it to enforce closed shapes is misguided.

q: What is the difference between `any` and `unknown`, and which should you prefer at an API boundary?
a: `any` opts out of type checking entirely: any operation is allowed and it silently spreads through the code. `unknown` is the type-safe top type: anything is assignable to it, but you cannot read properties or call it until you narrow or assert to a specific type. At boundaries like JSON.parse or a fetch response, prefer `unknown` so the compiler forces a guard before use.
fs: Prefer `unknown` at boundaries because it forces narrowing before any access, unlike `any` which disables checking.
fj: Treating them as interchangeable, or typing parsed/external data as `any` for convenience.

followups.q: What can you actually do with an `unknown` value without narrowing it first?
followups.a: Very little: you can assign it to another `unknown` or `any`, compare it with `===`/`==`, use it in `typeof`/`instanceof`/`in` guards, and pass it where `unknown` is expected. You cannot access properties, call it, index it, or do arithmetic. Every meaningful use requires narrowing or an assertion first, which is exactly the safety it buys.

followups.q: Why doesn't TypeScript automatically narrow `JSON.parse` results to a validated type, and what closes that gap at runtime?
followups.a: `JSON.parse` returns `any` by design because TS cannot know the parsed shape at compile time, and types are erased at runtime so no validation happens. The gap is closed with a runtime validator like `zod`, `valibot`, or a hand-written type guard; the validator's parse result gives you a properly typed value the compiler can trust.

followups.q: Is `unknown` absorbing in unions and intersections like `any` is?
followups.a: Partly. In a union, `unknown` absorbs everything: `unknown | T` collapses to `unknown`. In an intersection it is the identity: `unknown & T` reduces to `T`. This differs from `any`, which absorbs in unions but also poisons intersections to `any`, reflecting that `unknown` is the disciplined top type.

q: Explain narrowing and the main built-in type guards TypeScript uses.
a: Narrowing is the compiler's control-flow analysis refining a variable to a more specific type within a branch. The built-in guards are `typeof` for primitives, `instanceof` for class instances, the `in` operator for property presence, truthiness checks, and equality/discriminant comparisons. After a guard in a branch, the variable is typed as the narrowed type in that scope.
fs: Names control-flow analysis plus the four guard kinds (typeof, instanceof, in, equality) and knows truthiness narrows away null/undefined.
fj: Only knowing `typeof` and assuming you must cast with `as` to use a union member.

followups.q: Why does a narrowing achieved before a closure not hold inside the callback?
followups.a: Control-flow narrowing is invalidated across function boundaries because the compiler cannot prove the variable was not reassigned between narrowing and the deferred callback execution. Inside a closure the variable reverts to its declared type. The fix is to copy into a `const` (which narrows durably) or re-check inside the callback.

followups.q: Why is `typeof x === 'object'` insufficient to narrow away `null`?
followups.a: Because `typeof null === 'object'` is a long-standing JavaScript quirk, so the `object` branch still includes `null`. You must add an explicit `&& x !== null` (or a truthiness check) to exclude it. TS models this faithfully and will keep `null` in the type until you guard it out.

followups.q: How does reassigning a variable inside a branch affect prior narrowing?
followups.a: Narrowing is tied to the value at a program point, so any assignment re-runs control-flow analysis from the assigned expression's type. If you reassign to a wider value, the narrowing is lost from that point. This is also why `let` narrowing is more fragile than `const`, which can never be reassigned.

q: How do you write a user-defined type guard, and when is it needed over a built-in guard?
a: A user-defined type guard is a function whose return type is a type predicate of the form `param is Type`; when it returns true, TS narrows the argument to that type in the caller. You need it when the check is too complex for typeof/instanceof, for example validating the shape of an `unknown` value or distinguishing two interfaces structurally. The body must actually verify the predicate, since TS trusts the signature without checking the logic.
fs: Returns `param is Type` and is the right tool to narrow `unknown` or interface unions where built-in guards cannot.
fj: Returning `boolean` instead of a predicate, so the call type-checks but does not narrow the caller.

lines: function isCat(p: Pet): p is Cat {

followups.q: What is the danger of a type guard whose body does not actually verify the predicate?
followups.a: TS trusts the `is` signature without checking the implementation, so a guard that returns `true` for non-conforming values silently lies. Downstream code then accesses properties that may not exist at runtime, reintroducing the exact crashes types are meant to prevent. The guard is effectively an unchecked `as` cast dressed up as safe.

followups.q: What do TS 5.5 inferred type predicates change about writing guards?
followups.a: Since 5.5, TS can infer a type predicate for a simple boolean-returning function whose body is a clear narrowing check, so `arr.filter(x => x != null)` narrows the result without an explicit `is` annotation. You still write explicit predicates for complex validation, but trivial guards now narrow automatically, fixing the classic `filter(Boolean)` non-narrowing pain.

followups.q: How would you write a generic type guard that asserts an array is fully populated with a given type?
followups.a: Make the guard generic and return a predicate over the array type, e.g. `function isArrayOf<T>(a: unknown, g: (x: unknown) => x is T): a is T[]`. Inside, verify `Array.isArray(a)` then `a.every(g)`. This composes a per-element guard into a whole-array predicate, narrowing the caller to `T[]`.

q: What is a discriminated union and why is it preferred over a bag of optional fields?
a: A discriminated (tagged) union is a union of object types that all share a literal-typed property, the discriminant, whose distinct values let TS narrow the whole object when you switch on that property. It is preferred over one type with many optional fields because it makes illegal states unrepresentable: each variant carries exactly its own fields, so you cannot accidentally have a success value with an error field. Switching on the tag gives precise, type-safe access in each branch.
fs: Shared literal discriminant makes invalid combinations unrepresentable and drives exhaustive narrowing per variant.
fj: Modeling it as one interface with many optionals and runtime null-checks instead of a tag.

followups.q: Why must the discriminant be a literal type rather than a plain `string`?
followups.a: Narrowing works by matching the discriminant against a specific literal value, which only filters the union if each variant's tag is a distinct literal like `'ok'` vs `'err'`. If the tag were typed `string`, every comparison would be indeterminate and no variant could be ruled out, so `r.data` would never become accessible.

followups.q: Can a discriminated union use a boolean as its discriminant?
followups.a: Yes. A two-variant union keyed on `success: true` vs `success: false` is a valid discriminated union because `true` and `false` are distinct literal types. The classic `{ ok: true; value } | { ok: false; error }` Result type relies on exactly this, and switching/branching on the boolean narrows correctly.

followups.q: What goes wrong if two variants share the same discriminant value?
followups.a: Narrowing collapses: when you check the tag, TS keeps every variant carrying that value, so you only get access to their common members and lose per-variant precision. The discriminant must be unique per variant for the union to be properly discriminated; overlapping tags silently degrade it to a loose union.

q: How do you get a compile-time exhaustiveness check over a discriminated union?
a: In the default/else branch, assign the value to a variable of type `never` (commonly via an `assertNever(x: never): never` helper). When every variant is handled, the value is narrowed to `never` and the assignment compiles. If someone later adds a variant and forgets a case, the value is no longer `never`, so the assignment fails to compile, turning a silent runtime gap into a build error.
fs: Uses the `never` parameter of assertNever so adding a variant breaks the build until it is handled.
fj: Relying on a runtime default-case throw, which only fails after shipping rather than at compile time.

followups.q: How does `switch (true)` exhaustiveness differ from switching on a discriminant property?
followups.a: `switch (true)` with arbitrary boolean cases does not narrow the subject across cases, so the `default` value is not refined to `never` and exhaustiveness fails. The `assertNever` pattern only works when narrowing actually eliminates variants, which requires switching/branching directly on the literal discriminant.

followups.q: Why is a runtime `default: throw` insufficient compared to the `never` approach?
followups.a: A runtime throw only fires when an unhandled variant is actually reached during execution, so a forgotten case ships and fails in production. The `never` assignment fails at compile time the moment the union grows, catching the gap during the build before any code runs.

followups.q: Can you enforce exhaustiveness without a function, using `noImplicitReturns` or `satisfies`?
followups.a: Partly. `noImplicitReturns` forces every path to return but does not prove the union is covered. The cleaner alternative is the `never` helper, or in expression position assigning the result to a `const _exhaustive: never = r`. `satisfies` does not provide exhaustiveness; it validates shape, not branch coverage.

q: Why use generics, and what does adding a constraint like `<T extends ...>` buy you?
a: Generics let a function or type preserve and relate the caller's types instead of widening to `any` or a union, so the return type tracks the input. A constraint with `extends` restricts what `T` can be so you can safely use specific members inside the body while still being generic. Without the constraint you cannot access any property of `T`; with `T extends { id: number }` you can read `.id` and still return the precise input type.
fs: Constraints let you use members of T safely while keeping the precise input-to-output type relationship.
fj: Using `any` parameters and return, or over-constraining so the generic loses its caller's exact type.

followups.q: What is the difference between a constrained generic `<T extends Shape>(x: T)` and just typing the parameter `(x: Shape)`?
followups.a: The plain parameter widens the argument to `Shape`, losing the caller's exact type, so the return cannot reference specifics. The generic captures the precise input type and can flow it to the return or relate multiple parameters. Use a generic only when the type relationship matters; otherwise a plain parameter is simpler.

followups.q: How does a default type parameter `<T = string>` interact with a constraint?
followups.a: You can combine them as `<T extends string = string>`: the constraint bounds what callers may supply, and the default is used when `T` cannot be inferred and is not given explicitly. The default must itself satisfy the constraint, or it is a compile error. Defaults are common in library APIs to make the common case ergonomic.

followups.q: Why can over-constraining a generic be as harmful as under-constraining it?
followups.a: An overly tight constraint forces unnecessary widening or rejects valid callers, defeating the point of the generic. For example constraining to a concrete interface when only one property is used prevents passing structurally-compatible-but-unrelated types. Constrain to the minimal shape the body actually requires, no more.

q: What does `keyof` combined with a generic key parameter let you express in a typed getter?
a: Declaring `<T, K extends keyof T>` constrains `K` to the actual property names of `T`, so a getter `(obj: T, key: K) => T[K]` returns the precise property type via the indexed access `T[K]`. This rejects invalid keys at compile time and avoids a widened return type. It is the canonical type-safe property-access pattern and underpins many utility types.
fs: `K extends keyof T` plus indexed access `T[K]` gives a getter that rejects bad keys and returns the exact field type.
fj: Typing the key as `string` and the return as `any`, losing both safety and precision.

lines: function get<T, K extends keyof T>(

followups.q: What does `keyof` produce for a type with an index signature like `Record<string, number>`?
followups.a: `keyof` yields `string | number` (number because JS coerces numeric keys to strings), not a union of literal names, since the index signature admits any string key. For an object with explicit named properties you get a union of those literal keys instead. Mixing both gives the union of literals plus the index key type.

followups.q: Why does indexed access `T[K]` distribute over a union key `K`?
followups.a: When `K` is a union like `'a' | 'b'`, `T[K]` resolves to the union of each property's type, `T['a'] | T['b']`. This distribution is what makes `Pick` and value-extraction utilities work, and it is why `T[keyof T]` gives you the union of all value types in `T`.

followups.q: How does `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` change the return of such a getter when `T` has an index signature?
followups.a: With that flag on, indexed access through an index signature yields `T[K] | undefined`, reflecting that the key may be absent at runtime. For a known literal key on a declared property the result is unchanged. It pushes you to handle the possibly-missing case that bare indexing silently ignores.

q: What is a conditional type, and what does `infer` do inside one?
a: A conditional type has the form `T extends U ? X : Y`, branching at the type level on assignability. `infer` introduces a fresh type variable inside the `extends` clause that TS solves from the matched structure, usable in the true branch. For example `T extends Array<infer E> ? E : never` extracts the element type; this is how built-ins like `ReturnType` and `Awaited` pull a type out of a larger one.
fs: Knows conditional types branch on assignability and `infer` captures a sub-type from the matched shape (e.g. array element, return type).
fj: Confusing type-level `extends` with runtime checks, or thinking `infer` can be used outside a conditional type.

lines: T extends Array<infer E> ? E : never

followups.q: What is a distributive conditional type, and how do you stop the distribution?
followups.a: When the checked type is a naked type parameter and you pass a union, the conditional distributes over each member: `T extends U ? X : Y` applied to `A | B` becomes `(A extends U...) | (B extends U...)`. To evaluate the union as a whole, wrap both sides in tuples: `[T] extends [U] ? X : Y`, which defeats distribution.

followups.q: What happens when multiple `infer` sites for the same variable match different types?
followups.a: In covariant positions TS infers the union of the candidates; in contravariant positions (like function parameters) it infers the intersection. This is why `ReturnType` of an overloaded function picks the last overload's return, and why inferring a parameter type across positions can yield an intersection.

followups.q: Can you add a constraint to an `infer` variable, and why is that useful?
followups.a: Yes, since TS 4.7 you can write `infer E extends string`, which both constrains the match and, in newer versions, narrows the inferred type. It lets you require the captured type to satisfy a bound and avoids a separate nested conditional, making extraction utilities tighter and more readable.

q: What do `Partial`, `Pick`, `Omit`, and `Record` do, and how are they built?
a: `Partial<T>` makes every property optional (a mapped type with a `?` modifier); `Pick<T, K>` keeps only keys `K`; `Omit<T, K>` keeps all keys except `K` (built as `Pick<T, Exclude<keyof T, K>>`); `Record<K, V>` builds an object type with keys `K` and value type `V`. They are mapped/conditional types in lib.es5, so they stay in sync with `T` automatically. Deriving types from a source type with these avoids drift between related shapes.
fs: Knows they are mapped types over keyof and derives related shapes from one source type instead of redeclaring.
fj: Hand-rewriting a parallel interface for updates instead of `Partial<T>`, so the two drift apart.

lines: type User = { id: number; name: string }

followups.q: Why is `Omit` considered weaker than `Pick`, and what does it not protect against?
followups.a: `Omit<T, K>` does not constrain `K` to `keyof T` (its key parameter is `keyof any`), so omitting a misspelled or nonexistent key silently succeeds and produces no error. `Pick` and `Exclude` are stricter. This is why typo'd keys in `Omit` are a classic refactor hazard that survives renames.

followups.q: Why is `Partial<T>` dangerous to use as a function parameter for in-place updates?
followups.a: `Partial` makes every field optional including ones an update must not blank out, and it does not distinguish 'absent' from 'explicitly undefined' unless `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` is on. It can also mask required invariants, letting a caller pass `{}`. For updates, model a narrower patch type or a discriminated set of allowed changes.

followups.q: Does `Partial<T>` recurse into nested objects?
followups.a: No, it is shallow: only top-level properties become optional, while nested object types stay fully required. A deep-optional version requires a recursive mapped type (`DeepPartial`) that maps each object-valued property through itself. Assuming `Partial` is deep is a common source of type errors on nested config.

q: When should you reach for `ReturnType` and `Awaited` rather than declaring a return type by hand?
a: `ReturnType<typeof fn>` extracts a function's return type via `infer`, and `Awaited<T>` recursively unwraps the type a Promise resolves to (modeling `await`). Use them to derive a type from an existing implementation so it stays correct as that implementation evolves, instead of maintaining a duplicate hand-written type. They are the idiomatic way to type, say, the resolved payload of an async data loader.
fs: Derives types from the implementation with ReturnType/Awaited so they cannot drift from the real function.
fj: Hand-copying the resolved shape and silently going stale when the function's return changes.

followups.q: Why does `Awaited` recurse rather than unwrap a single Promise layer?
followups.a: Because `await` itself flattens nested thenables: awaiting a `Promise<Promise<T>>` yields `T`, not `Promise<T>`. `Awaited` mirrors this by recursively unwrapping any value with a `then` method until it reaches a non-thenable, so the type matches actual runtime behavior of chained promises.

followups.q: What does `ReturnType` give you for an overloaded function, and why?
followups.a: It resolves to the return type of the last overload signature, because conditional-type inference against an overloaded function type matches the final implementation-facing signature. This is a known gotcha: if you need a specific overload's return, you must construct the call type explicitly rather than relying on `ReturnType`.

followups.q: When would deriving with `ReturnType<typeof fn>` create an unwanted coupling?
followups.a: When the function's return is an implementation detail you do not want to expose as a contract, deriving from it leaks internal shape into your public types and makes refactors ripple outward. In that case an explicit, intentionally-stable interface is better, and the function should be declared to return it.

q: When would you choose an `interface` over a `type` alias, and vice versa?
a: Use `interface` for object/class shapes and public API surfaces: it supports declaration merging and `extends`, which library authors rely on to let consumers augment types. Use `type` for anything that is not a plain object shape: unions, intersections, tuples, primitives, mapped and conditional types, since aliases can express those and interfaces cannot. They are largely interchangeable for object shapes; the merging behavior and union/conditional capability are the real differentiators.
fs: Interface for mergeable/extendable object APIs; type alias for unions, tuples, mapped and conditional types.
fj: Claiming they are identical, or trying to write a union with `interface`.

followups.q: Is there any performance difference between `interface extends` and intersection `type` for large shapes?
followups.a: Yes, subtly. `interface extends` creates a flat, cached resolved type the compiler can check against efficiently, whereas deep intersections (`A & B & C`) are recomputed and can be slower and produce worse error messages on large unions. For extending object shapes in hot type paths, `interface extends` is the more performant choice.

followups.q: Why does declaration merging on interfaces cut both ways as a footgun?
followups.a: It is the feature that enables module augmentation, but two same-named interfaces in scope silently merge instead of erroring, so an accidental name collision combines members and produces a confusing super-type. Type aliases error on redeclaration, which is safer when you do not want extensibility.

followups.q: Can an `interface` extend a `type` alias and vice versa?
followups.a: An `interface` can `extends` a `type` alias only if the alias resolves to an object-like (statically known) shape; it cannot extend a union or a type computed by a conditional. A `type` can intersect with an interface freely. So aliases are the more flexible base, but interfaces give the cleaner extension semantics for plain objects.

q: How does `Promise.all` differ from `Promise.allSettled`, and how does that shape your error handling?
a: `Promise.all` rejects (short-circuits) as soon as any input promise rejects, so `await Promise.all(...)` throws and you lose the results that did succeed. `Promise.allSettled` never rejects; it resolves to an array of `{ status: 'fulfilled', value }` or `{ status: 'rejected', reason }`, so you inspect each outcome. Use `all` for all-or-nothing dependent work and `allSettled` for independent tasks where partial success matters.
fs: all short-circuits on first rejection; allSettled returns a typed status array so partial failures are recoverable.
fj: Assuming `Promise.all` waits for everything and returns errors per item, or that `allSettled` can reject.

followups.q: Does `Promise.all` rejecting actually cancel the other in-flight promises?
followups.a: No. Promises are not cancelable, so the other operations keep running to completion; you simply stop awaiting their results. This can leak resources or cause unhandled rejections if those promises later reject. For true cancellation you need an `AbortController` threaded into each task.

followups.q: How does the result type of `Promise.all` preserve per-element types for a tuple of promises?
followups.a: `Promise.all` has tuple-aware overloads so `Promise.all([p1, p2] as const)`-style heterogeneous inputs resolve to a typed tuple `[T1, T2]`, not a widened `(T1 | T2)[]`. This lets you destructure with correct types per position, which is the main typing advantage over manually awaiting.

followups.q: When would `Promise.race` or `Promise.any` be the right choice over these two?
followups.a: Use `Promise.race` when the first settled outcome (fulfilled or rejected) should win, e.g. a timeout pattern. Use `Promise.any` when you want the first fulfillment and only reject (with an `AggregateError`) if all fail, e.g. querying redundant mirrors. They answer 'fastest one' rather than 'all of them'.

q: Under modern config, what is the type of the variable in a `catch` clause, and why?
a: With `useUnknownInCatchVariables` (on under `strict` since TS 4.4), the catch binding is `unknown`, not `any`, because anything can be thrown in JavaScript, not just `Error`. You must narrow before using it, typically `e instanceof Error` before reading `.message`. This prevents the common bug of assuming a thrown value has `.message` or `.stack` when it might be a string, number, or arbitrary object.
fs: Catch variable is `unknown` under strict, so guard with `instanceof Error` before touching `.message`.
fj: Reading `e.message` directly, assuming everything thrown is an `Error` instance.

followups.q: Why can `e instanceof Error` fail across realms even when the value is genuinely an Error?
followups.a: `instanceof` checks the prototype chain against a specific `Error` constructor, but a value created in another realm (an iframe, a worker, or a different VM context) has a different `Error.prototype`, so the check returns false. In those cases a duck-typed check (`typeof e === 'object' && 'message' in e`) is more robust.

followups.q: How do you write a reusable helper to extract a message from an `unknown` thrown value safely?
followups.a: Write `getErrorMessage(e: unknown): string` that returns `e.message` if `e instanceof Error`, falls back to checking `'message' in e` for error-like objects, and otherwise `String(e)` or `JSON.stringify`. Centralizing this avoids repeating the narrowing at every catch site and handles non-Error throws uniformly.

followups.q: Can you annotate a catch variable explicitly, and what are you allowed to write?
followups.a: Since TS 4.0 you may annotate it, but only as `unknown` or `any`; any other annotation is a syntax error because the runtime gives you whatever was thrown. Annotating `: unknown` is mostly redundant under `useUnknownInCatchVariables`, but useful for clarity or to opt into the safer type when the flag is off.

q: Why is typing a function as `throws`-free and returning a Result type sometimes preferable to throwing in TypeScript?
a: TypeScript has no checked exceptions: a function's signature does not record what it might throw, so callers get no compile-time signal to handle errors. Returning a discriminated Result like `{ ok: true; value } | { ok: false; error }` puts failure in the type system, forcing the caller to narrow on `ok` before reading `value`. This makes error paths explicit and exhaustively checkable, at the cost of more verbose call sites; throwing remains fine for truly exceptional, unrecoverable cases.
fs: Notes TS has no checked exceptions, so a Result union moves failure into the type system the caller must narrow.
fj: Believing the function signature documents thrown errors, or wrapping everything in try/catch and ignoring types.

followups.q: What is the main ergonomic cost of Result types, and how do libraries mitigate it?
followups.a: Every call site must unwrap and the failure type does not auto-propagate the way `throw` does, so chains get verbose. Libraries like `neverthrow` add combinators (`map`, `andThen`, `mapErr`) to thread results without manual narrowing at each step, recovering railway-style composition while keeping failure in the type.

followups.q: Why doesn't returning `T | Error` work as well as a tagged Result?
followups.a: `T | Error` is fragile: if `T` is an object, an `Error` is structurally a possible `T` member, and narrowing relies on `instanceof Error` which breaks across realms or for custom error shapes. A tagged `ok` discriminant narrows reliably and works even when the success and failure payloads have overlapping shapes.

followups.q: When is throwing genuinely the better choice despite the lack of checked exceptions?
followups.a: For programmer errors and truly unrecoverable conditions (invariant violations, out-of-memory, misconfiguration at startup) where local handling is meaningless and you want a stack trace to propagate to a top-level boundary. Result types shine for expected, recoverable failures like validation or I/O the caller must branch on.

q: What problem does `import type` solve, and what does `verbatimModuleSyntax` enforce?
a: `import type` marks an import as type-only so it is fully erased at emit and never produces a runtime `require`/`import`, which avoids accidental side-effect loads and circular-dependency cycles from type-only references. `verbatimModuleSyntax` (TS 5.0, the recommended successor to `importsNotUsedAsValues`/`isolatedModules` heuristics) enforces this explicitly: any import without `type` is emitted verbatim, and anything with `type` is dropped, so you must annotate type-only imports. This gives single-file transpilers like esbuild or SWC a deterministic erasure rule.
fs: `import type` erases at emit; verbatimModuleSyntax makes the type/value distinction explicit so emit is deterministic.
fj: Thinking unused type imports are always elided automatically, ignoring runtime side effects and bundler behavior.

followups.q: How does `verbatimModuleSyntax` interact with CommonJS output and `export =`?
followups.a: Because it emits module syntax verbatim, it does not rewrite ESM import/export into CommonJS `require`, so in a CommonJS-targeted project you must use `import x = require('...')` and `export =` forms. Mixing ESM syntax under `verbatimModuleSyntax` with a CommonJS module target will error, forcing you to pick a consistent module system.

followups.q: When would you use inline `import { type Foo, bar }` versus a separate `import type` statement?
followups.a: Inline `type` modifiers let you mix type-only and value imports from one module in a single statement, dropping only `Foo` at emit while keeping `bar`. A separate `import type` line is for when every binding is type-only. Both satisfy `verbatimModuleSyntax`; inline is just more concise when a module gives you both kinds.

followups.q: Why does `import type` help break circular dependencies specifically?
followups.a: Because it is erased entirely, a type-only import generates no runtime module load, so it cannot participate in an initialization cycle. Converting a value import (used only for its type) to `import type` removes the runtime edge from the dependency graph, resolving cycles that caused `undefined` exports at load time.

q: What does `isolatedModules` restrict, and why do transpilers like Babel/esbuild require it?
a: `isolatedModules` tells TS to flag code that cannot be correctly transpiled one file at a time without whole-program type information. The main offenders are re-exporting a type without `export type` (the single-file tool can't tell type from value), `const enum` usage (which needs cross-file inlining), and certain namespace patterns. Single-file transpilers compile each module in isolation for speed, so they need these guarantees that `tsc`'s type-aware emit would otherwise provide.
fs: Flags type-only re-exports and const enums that a per-file transpiler cannot resolve without the whole program.
fj: Assuming any tool that runs TS handles `const enum` and bare type re-exports the same as `tsc`.

followups.q: How does `isolatedModules` relate to `verbatimModuleSyntax`; do you need both?
followups.a: They overlap but differ: `isolatedModules` flags constructs unsafe for per-file transpilation, while `verbatimModuleSyntax` additionally requires explicit `type` markers and emits imports verbatim. Modern projects targeting esbuild/swc typically enable `verbatimModuleSyntax`, which subsumes much of the import-related guidance; `isolatedModules` remains valuable for the broader set of warnings.

followups.q: Why does `const enum` specifically fail under `isolatedModules`?
followups.a: A `const enum` is inlined by replacing each member reference with its literal value, which requires the transpiler to read the enum's declaration from another file. A single-file transpiler sees only the current module and cannot resolve the value, so `isolatedModules` forbids ambient/cross-file `const enum` use to prevent broken output.

followups.q: Does `isolatedModules` affect whether you can use `export default` of a type?
followups.a: You cannot `export default` a bare type and re-import it as a value safely, and `isolatedModules` surfaces the ambiguities. More generally it pushes you to keep type and value exports distinguishable per file, so default-exporting types is discouraged in favor of named `export type` declarations.

q: What does turning on `strictNullChecks` change, and why is it worth the migration cost?
a: With `strictNullChecks` on, `null` and `undefined` are no longer assignable to every type; they only belong to `null`, `undefined`, and `any`, so a `string` cannot silently be `null`. You must include them in the type (`string | null`) and narrow before use, which surfaces the whole class of 'cannot read property of undefined' bugs at compile time. It is the single highest-value strict flag and is part of the `strict` family.
fs: Makes null/undefined explicit members of the type so absent-value bugs become compile errors before runtime.
fj: Leaving it off because it is noisy, then hitting null dereference crashes the compiler could have caught.

lines: // s.length // Error: s may be null

followups.q: What is the difference between optional `?` and explicitly typing `| undefined` under `strictNullChecks`?
followups.a: An optional property `x?: T` may be absent entirely and is typed `T | undefined`, whereas `x: T | undefined` requires the key to be present but allows the value `undefined`. With `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`, that distinction is enforced: you cannot assign explicit `undefined` to an `x?: T` property without including `undefined` in its type.

followups.q: How do the `?.` and `??` operators complement `strictNullChecks`?
followups.a: Optional chaining `a?.b` short-circuits to `undefined` when `a` is null/undefined, and nullish coalescing `a ?? d` supplies a default only for null/undefined (not other falsy values). Under `strictNullChecks` the compiler tracks that `?.` introduces `undefined` into the type, so you must still handle the resulting `T | undefined` downstream.

followups.q: What is the recommended strategy for migrating a large codebase to `strictNullChecks` incrementally?
followups.a: Enable it globally but use a tool or per-file `// @ts-nocheck`/staged ratchet to fix modules one at a time, often bottom-up from leaf utilities. Some teams use `strictNullChecks` per-directory via project references or a separate tsconfig. The key is to avoid a giant unreviewable diff while preventing new violations in already-converted files.

q: When is the non-null assertion `!` acceptable, and what is its danger?
a: The `!` operator tells the compiler a value is not null/undefined without any runtime check, purely erasing the null from the type. It is acceptable only when an invariant guarantees presence that the compiler cannot see (e.g. a value set in an earlier lifecycle the type system doesn't track). The danger is that it is a silent lie: if you are wrong, you get a runtime crash with no compile-time warning, so a real guard, a default, or restructuring the types is usually safer.
fs: Treats `!` as an unchecked type-level override reserved for invariants the compiler cannot model, not a convenience.
fj: Sprinkling `!` to silence null errors, reintroducing exactly the crashes strictNullChecks prevents.

followups.q: What is the definite assignment assertion `!` on a property declaration, and how does it differ from the postfix `!`?
followups.a: Writing `private x!: number` tells the compiler the field is assigned before use even though no initializer is present, satisfying `strictPropertyInitialization`. It is a declaration-site assertion about initialization timing (common with DI or lifecycle hooks), whereas the postfix `expr!` asserts a specific expression is non-null at a use site.

followups.q: Why can `!` become stale and silently wrong after a refactor?
followups.a: It encodes an assumption that is invisible at the use site, so if a later change makes the value genuinely nullable, the `!` keeps suppressing the error instead of surfacing it. Unlike a real guard, it does not adapt, so it converts what should be a compile error into a latent runtime crash. Prefer guards that the compiler re-validates.

followups.q: How can a lint rule help you control `!` usage across a team?
followups.a: `@typescript-eslint/no-non-null-assertion` flags every `!` so usage is reviewed rather than habitual, and you can allow narrow exceptions via disable comments with justification. This keeps assertions deliberate and documented, turning an easy-to-abuse operator into one that requires explicit sign-off.

q: Explain how TypeScript infers types from initializers and contextual position, and where inference goes wrong.
a: TS infers a variable's type from its initializer and infers function parameter types contextually from the expected type at the call/assignment site (contextual typing). Two gotchas dominate: literal types widen, so `let x = 'a'` becomes `string` not `'a'` unless you use `const` or `as const`; and inferred array/object literal types may be broader than intended. Use `as const`, explicit annotations, or `satisfies` to pin literals while still validating against a type.
fs: Knows widening and contextual typing, and reaches for `as const`/`satisfies` to keep literals narrow without losing checking.
fj: Assuming `let x = 'a'` is the literal `'a'`, or annotating everything and discarding inference entirely.

followups.q: Why does `const b = 'x'` infer the literal `'x'` but `const o = { k: 'x' }` infer `{ k: string }`?
followups.a: Immutable `const` bindings of a primitive keep the literal type because the value can never change. Object properties are mutable even on a `const` object, so each property widens to its base type to allow reassignment. `as const` overrides this by making the properties `readonly`, which lets the literal types survive.

followups.q: What is contextual typing failing to do when you write `arr.map(x => x.id)` and `x` is `any`?
followups.a: It means `arr`'s element type was not inferable (often because `arr` itself is `any` or untyped), so there is no contextual type to flow into the callback parameter `x`. Contextual typing only works when the surrounding expected type is known; fix the source type of `arr` and `x` will be inferred correctly.

followups.q: How does best-common-type inference pick an array literal's type, and when is that surprising?
followups.a: TS computes the best common type across elements; if no single supertype fits, it forms a union, e.g. `[1, 'a']` becomes `(number | string)[]`. With class hierarchies it may pick a common base rather than a union, which can be surprising. Annotate the array or use `as const` when you need an exact tuple or a specific element type.

q: What does the `satisfies` operator give you that a type annotation does not?
a: `satisfies` checks that an expression conforms to a type without widening or replacing the expression's own inferred type, so you keep the precise literal types while still validating the shape. A plain annotation (`const x: T = ...`) forces the value to `T`, discarding narrower inference like specific keys or literal values. It is the idiomatic way to validate a config object against an interface while preserving exact key and value types for downstream inference.
fs: `satisfies` validates against a type but retains the value's narrow inferred type; annotation widens to the declared type.
fj: Using `as T` to 'check' a literal, which is an unsafe cast that can hide mismatches rather than validate.

followups.q: How is `satisfies` fundamentally safer than `as` for validating a literal?
followups.a: `satisfies` performs a real assignability check and errors if the expression does not conform, while keeping the narrow inferred type. `as` is a one-way assertion that forces the type with no check (within reason), so it can silence genuine mismatches and even mask missing properties. `satisfies` validates; `as` overrides.

followups.q: Can you combine `satisfies` with `as const`, and why would you?
followups.a: Yes: `({ ... } as const) satisfies Config`. The `as const` pins literals and makes the object `readonly`, and `satisfies` then verifies the frozen shape matches `Config` without widening. This is the common pattern for a strongly-typed, immutable config whose exact key/value literals you still want to flow into inference.

followups.q: When does a plain annotation `: T` still beat `satisfies`?
followups.a: When you specifically want the variable's type to BE `T` for the rest of the program, e.g. to enforce a uniform interface and hide implementation specifics, or to get excess-property checking and a stable public type. `satisfies` keeps the narrow concrete type, which is undesirable when you want the abstraction the declared type provides.

q: What is the difference between a union type and an intersection type, and what does each mean for the members you can access?
a: A union `A | B` means the value is one of the types, so you may only access members common to all members until you narrow; it is the type-level OR. An intersection `A & B` means the value satisfies both at once, so it has all members of A and all of B combined; it is the type-level AND. Unions model alternatives (a result that is success or failure), while intersections compose shapes (mixing in extra fields). Intersecting incompatible primitives like `string & number` yields `never`.
fs: Union is one-of (only common members until narrowed); intersection is both-at-once (all members combined).
fj: Thinking `A | B` gives you all members of both, or that `&` means either one.

lines: type Id = string | number // one of
type P = Named & Aged // has both

followups.q: What happens when you intersect two object types that declare the same property with incompatible types?
followups.a: The property type becomes the intersection of the two, which for incompatible primitives is `never`. So `{ x: string } & { x: number }` has `x: never`, meaning no value can ever satisfy it. The overall type is not rejected outright, but the property is uninhabitable, which is a subtle way an intersection becomes effectively impossible.

followups.q: Why can calling a function typed as a union of function types be problematic?
followups.a: A union of functions can only be called with arguments valid for ALL members (the intersection of their parameter types), because the compiler does not know which one you hold. This often produces a `never` parameter or an error. Intersections of functions, by contrast, behave like overloads and are callable with either signature.

followups.q: How does narrowing a union actually grant access to a non-common member?
followups.a: A type guard eliminates union members in a branch, leaving only those that have the member, so the property becomes accessible. Without narrowing, TS restricts you to the members present on every constituent because the value could be any of them. The whole point of discriminants and guards is to safely collapse the union to a member.

q: What does a `const` assertion (`as const`) do to an object and array literal?
a: `as const` tells TS not to widen the literal: string and number literals keep their exact literal type instead of `string`/`number`, object properties become `readonly`, and array literals become `readonly` tuples with fixed length and element types. This is how you turn a plain array into a precise tuple or freeze a config object so its literal values flow into inference. It is a compile-time annotation only; it adds no runtime `Object.freeze`.
fs: `as const` blocks widening: literals stay exact, objects become readonly, arrays become readonly tuples.
fj: Expecting `as const` to deep-freeze at runtime, or thinking it only affects the outermost value.

followups.q: How deeply does `as const` apply; does it affect nested objects and arrays?
followups.a: It is deep at the type level: nested objects become `readonly` and nested arrays become `readonly` tuples all the way down, and every literal stays narrow throughout the structure. This is unlike `Object.freeze`, which is shallow. So a deeply nested config literal is fully pinned and immutable in the type system with a single `as const`.

followups.q: Why does `as const` add no runtime immutability, and how do you get both?
followups.a: Types are erased at compile time, so `readonly` is a compile-time-only contract and the object is still mutable at runtime. To enforce runtime immutability you must call `Object.freeze` (shallow) or a deep-freeze utility in addition. They are complementary: `as const` for type precision, `freeze` for actual runtime protection.

followups.q: How does `as const` enable deriving a literal union with `typeof X[number]`?
followups.a: Because `as const` turns an array into a `readonly` tuple of literals, indexing that tuple type with `[number]` yields the union of its element literal types. So `const Roles = ['a','b'] as const; type Role = typeof Roles[number]` gives `'a' | 'b'`. Without `as const`, the element type would widen to `string` and the union would collapse.

q: Why would you choose a union of string literals over a TypeScript `enum`?
a: A string-literal union like `'asc' | 'desc'` is purely a type: it erases completely, adds zero runtime code, and narrows naturally with `===`. A regular `enum` emits a runtime object (an IIFE with reverse mappings) that bundlers often cannot tree-shake, and numeric enums allow any number to be assigned. Unions are also easier to derive with `keyof`/`typeof` from an `as const` object. Reach for `enum` only when you genuinely want a named runtime namespace of values to iterate.
fs: Literal unions are erased and tree-shakeable; enums emit a runtime object and numeric ones are loosely typed.
fj: Assuming enums have no runtime cost, or that a numeric enum rejects arbitrary numbers.

lines: // derive a union from a const obj:

followups.q: What capability do you lose by using a literal union instead of an enum?
followups.a: You lose a runtime value to iterate or reference: an enum gives a real object you can loop over, get all members from, and use as a namespace. With a union you must keep a parallel `as const` array (`Roles`) to enumerate values at runtime and derive the type from it. The union itself has no runtime presence to iterate.

followups.q: Why does `erasableSyntaxOnly` (TS 5.8) push teams toward literal unions?
followups.a: Because `enum` emits runtime code and is therefore non-erasable, it is banned under `erasableSyntaxOnly`, which type-stripping runtimes like Node's native TS support require. Literal unions plus an `as const` array are fully erasable, so they run under any erase-only loader. This makes the union pattern the future-proof default.

followups.q: How do you bridge a literal union to a runtime value list without duplication risk?
followups.a: Define the values once as an `as const` array and derive the type from it: `const Dirs = ['asc','desc'] as const; type Dir = typeof Dirs[number]`. The array is your single source of truth for both runtime iteration and the compile-time union, so they cannot drift. This is the idiomatic enum replacement.

q: What does `const enum` do differently from a regular enum, and why can it be risky?
a: A `const enum` is fully inlined at compile time: each member reference is replaced by its literal value and no runtime enum object is emitted, so it has zero runtime footprint. The risk is that inlining needs whole-program knowledge, so it breaks under single-file transpilers (Babel, esbuild) and `isolatedModules`, and ambient `const enum`s shipped in `.d.ts` files are fragile across package boundaries. Modern guidance is to prefer literal unions over `const enum` for most cases, or enable `preserveConstEnums` if you must keep the object.
fs: `const enum` inlines members and emits nothing, but breaks under per-file transpilers and across .d.ts boundaries.
fj: Using `const enum` in a library's published types, assuming consumers' bundlers will inline it correctly.

lines: // no enum object exists at runtime

followups.q: What specifically breaks when a library ships an ambient `const enum` in its `.d.ts`?
followups.a: Consumers compiling with `isolatedModules` or a non-`tsc` transpiler cannot inline a value they only see in a declaration file, so references either error or, worse, emit a reference to a non-existent runtime object. If the library later changes member values, already-inlined consumer code silently holds stale numbers. This is why ambient const enums are discouraged in published packages.

followups.q: What does `preserveConstEnums` do, and when would you enable it?
followups.a: It tells `tsc` to still emit the enum object even for `const enum`, so the runtime object exists for debugging or for code that references it dynamically, while inlining still occurs. You enable it when you need the const enum's compile-time inlining but also require the runtime object, e.g. for reflection or interop, accepting the extra emitted code.

followups.q: How does `const enum` behave under `erasableSyntaxOnly`?
followups.a: Like regular `enum`, it is non-erasable as a declaration (it introduces enum syntax that type-strippers do not understand), so it is disallowed under `erasableSyntaxOnly`. The recommended replacement is a literal union or an `as const` object, which are fully erasable and avoid the cross-file inlining hazards entirely.

q: What is a mapped type, and what do the `?`/`readonly` modifiers (and their `-` forms) control?
a: A mapped type iterates over a union of keys to build a new object type: `{ [K in keyof T]: T[K] }`. You can add or strip modifiers per property: `readonly`/`+readonly` makes fields immutable and `-readonly` removes immutability, while `?`/`+?` makes them optional and `-?` makes them required. This is how the standard library builds `Partial` (adds `?`) and how you write `Mutable<T>` (`-readonly`) or `Required<T>` (`-?`). Mapped types keep derived shapes in lockstep with their source.
fs: Mapped types remap keys and can add/remove `readonly` and `?`; `-readonly`/`-?` strip those modifiers.
fj: Thinking modifiers can only be added, not removed, so writing a `Mutable<T>` by hand instead of `-readonly`.

followups.q: Why is a hand-written `Mutable<T>` shallow, and how would you make it deep?
followups.a: The mapped type only strips `readonly` from top-level keys; nested object types keep their own `readonly` modifiers. A deep version recurses: map each property, and if its type is an object, apply `DeepMutable` to it via a conditional type. The same shallowness applies to `Partial`, `Required`, and `Readonly`.

followups.q: What is a homomorphic mapped type, and why does the `keyof T` form matter?
followups.a: A mapped type of the form `{ [K in keyof T]: ... }` is homomorphic: it preserves `T`'s modifiers and, when applied to arrays/tuples, maps over elements rather than turning them into plain objects. Using `keyof T` directly (not an arbitrary union) is what triggers this structure-preserving behavior, which is why `Partial` of an array stays array-like.

followups.q: Can mapped types map over a union of string literals that is not `keyof` an object?
followups.a: Yes: `{ [K in 'a' | 'b']: number }` builds `{ a: number; b: number }` from a literal key union directly. This non-homomorphic form is how `Record` is defined. It does not preserve source modifiers (there is no source) and is the building block for constructing object types from an arbitrary key set.

q: What is key remapping in a mapped type, and how do you use it to rename or filter keys?
a: Key remapping uses an `as` clause inside a mapped type to compute a new key from each original key: `{ [K in keyof T as NewKey]: ... }`. Combined with template literal types you can rename keys (e.g. prefix every key with `get`), and by remapping a key to `never` you drop it entirely, which is how you filter properties by name or value type. This makes mapped types a full key-transformation tool, not just a per-property modifier.
fs: An `as` clause remaps each key; template literals rename and remapping to `never` filters keys out.
fj: Believing mapped types can only keep the same key names, so reaching for conditional gymnastics to rename.

followups.q: How exactly does remapping a key to `never` remove it from the resulting type?
followups.a: When the `as` expression evaluates to `never` for a key, that key is excluded from the mapped type's output entirely. You typically use a conditional like `as T[K] extends Function ? K : never` to filter by value type. The keys producing `never` simply do not appear, which is the idiom for property filtering.

followups.q: Why is `Capitalize<string & K>` needed instead of just `Capitalize<K>` in the example?
followups.a: `K` from `keyof T` can be `string | number | symbol`, but template literal interpolation and `Capitalize` require a string. Intersecting with `string` (`string & K`) narrows `K` to its string members so it is a valid template operand. Without it, symbol/number keys would cause an error in the template literal type.

followups.q: How would you filter a type to only its function-valued properties using key remapping?
followups.a: Map over `keyof T` and remap each key conditionally: `{ [K in keyof T as T[K] extends Function ? K : never]: T[K] }`. Keys whose value is not a function remap to `never` and drop out, leaving only the method-like properties. This is the canonical pattern for extracting methods or any value-type-filtered subset.

q: What are template literal types and where are they genuinely useful?
a: Template literal types build string-literal types from other types using backtick syntax in type position, expanding unions combinatorially: `` `on${Capitalize<Event>}` `` produces a union of event-handler names. They power precise typing of string-keyed APIs, CSS-in-JS property names, route patterns, and event maps, and pair with the intrinsic string types `Uppercase`, `Lowercase`, `Capitalize`, and `Uncapitalize`. They are the type-level equivalent of string interpolation, checked at compile time.
fs: Template literal types interpolate and expand string unions, enabling typed key/route/event-name patterns.
fj: Treating them as runtime string formatting rather than a compile-time type that expands over unions.

followups.q: Why can a template literal type over multiple large unions cause a compiler explosion?
followups.a: Each `${Union}` slot expands combinatorially, so two unions of N and M members produce N*M literal types, and several slots multiply further. TS caps the size of unions it will materialize (around 100k members) and errors past it. Keep interpolated unions small, or use `${string}` for the open-ended part to avoid the blowup.

followups.q: How can template literal types parse a string, e.g. extract path parameters from a route?
followups.a: Combined with `infer` in a conditional type, a template literal can destructure a string type: `T extends \`${string}:${infer P}/${infer Rest}\`` captures segments. Recursing over `Rest` extracts all `:param` names into a union. This is how typed routers derive params like `{ id: string }` from a literal route path.

followups.q: What do the intrinsic types `Uppercase`/`Capitalize` do that you cannot replicate in user code?
followups.a: They perform compile-time string-case transformation that is not expressible with ordinary type operators, so the compiler implements them intrinsically. They let template literal types produce correctly-cased keys (like `onClick`) deterministically. There is no way to hand-write case conversion at the type level without these built-ins.

q: What does the `const` type parameter (`<const T>`) added in TS 5.0 do?
a: Marking a generic parameter `const` makes calls infer the argument as if the caller wrote `as const`, so literal types and tuple/readonly shapes are preserved without the caller adding `as const` at the call site. It is most useful for builder-style or config functions where you want exact literal keys and values to flow into the return type. It does not work on arrow-function type parameters and only affects inference, not the runtime value.
fs: `<const T>` infers arguments as if `as const` were written, preserving literals/tuples without caller boilerplate.
fj: Confusing `<const T>` with `T extends const` (not valid) or expecting it to freeze the value at runtime.

followups.q: Why doesn't `<const T>` make the runtime value immutable?
followups.a: It is purely an inference hint that mimics `as const` at the type level; types are erased, so no `Object.freeze` or `readonly` enforcement happens at runtime. The argument object remains mutable. It only changes how TS infers the literal/tuple type of the argument, not the value's runtime behavior.

followups.q: When does a caller's `as const` still beat a `<const T>` parameter?
followups.a: When the function author did not mark the parameter `const`, the caller must add `as const` to preserve literals. Also, if you need readonly-ness to propagate through later assignments at the call site (not just into this call's inference), an explicit `as const` on the value is clearer and travels with the value beyond this one function.

followups.q: What is the limitation around arrow functions and `const` type parameters?
followups.a: The `const` modifier on type parameters is not supported on arrow function type-parameter lists in the same way (it was a parser/feature limitation), so you generally apply it on function declarations or method signatures. If you need the behavior with an arrow, you may have to restructure to a function declaration or push the `const` requirement onto the caller.

q: What is the `never` type, and where does it legitimately show up?
a: `never` is the empty type: no value is assignable to it, and it is the return type of a function that never returns normally (it throws or loops forever). It appears as the result of impossible narrowing (the `default` branch where every union member is handled), as the element type of an empty intersection like `string & number`, and as a filtered-out key in mapped types. Because it is assignable to every type, you can use it as the parameter of an exhaustiveness helper to force compile-time coverage.
fs: `never` is the uninhabited type: no values, the return of throw/infinite functions, and the marker of impossible states.
fj: Confusing `never` (no values, bottom type) with `void` (returns undefined) or `unknown` (the top type).

followups.q: Why does `never` vanish from a union, e.g. `string | never` becoming `string`?
followups.a: Because `never` is the identity element for union (the bottom type): adding 'no possible values' to a set of values contributes nothing, so it is absorbed. This is intentional and is why conditional/mapped types that produce `never` for some branches cleanly filter those out of a resulting union.

followups.q: How is `never` different from `void` for a function return?
followups.a: `void` means the function returns (normally) but the return value should be ignored, typically `undefined`. `never` means the function never returns at all, because it throws or loops forever, so code after a `never`-returning call is unreachable. A function that returns `undefined` is `void`, not `never`.

followups.q: Why does a distributive conditional type over `never` produce `never` rather than iterating?
followups.a: `never` is the empty union, so distributing a conditional over it has zero members to map and yields `never`. This is occasionally surprising: passing `never` to a distributive utility short-circuits to `never` instead of the 'false branch'. Wrapping in a tuple (`[T] extends [never]`) lets you detect and special-case the `never` input.

q: What is declaration merging in TypeScript, and which declarations can merge?
a: Declaration merging is the compiler combining multiple declarations with the same name in the same scope into one. Interfaces with the same name merge their members, a namespace can merge with a function, class, or enum to add static members, and an enum can merge across declarations. Type aliases do not merge, which is one reason libraries expose interfaces. The most common deliberate use is augmenting an existing interface, while accidental merges (two same-named interfaces) can silently combine and surprise you.
fs: Same-named interfaces and namespaces merge into one; type aliases never merge, so interfaces are the extensible choice.
fj: Assuming two `type` aliases with the same name merge like interfaces, or that interface merging is an error.

lines: // Box now has w and h

followups.q: What happens if two merging interfaces declare the same property with conflicting types?
followups.a: Non-function properties must be identical across declarations or it is a compile error; you cannot merge `x: number` with `x: string`. Function members with different signatures, however, merge as overloads. So merging is permissive for methods (overload accumulation) but strict for plain property types.

followups.q: How does merging a namespace with a function or class extend it?
followups.a: A `namespace` with the same name as a function or class merges to attach static-like members onto it, e.g. adding `MyFn.helper` or nested types under a class. This is how the classic 'function plus its own properties' pattern is typed. The namespace members become accessible as properties of the function/class value.

followups.q: Why is ordering of merged interface members across files non-deterministic, and when does that bite?
followups.a: The compiler merges members in file-load order, which can vary, so overload resolution order for merged function members may differ between builds in edge cases. It rarely matters for properties, but for merged overloaded methods the resolution order can affect which signature matches first, making augmented overloads occasionally surprising.

q: How do you add a property to an existing library type, for example typing a custom field on Express's Request?
a: Use module augmentation: in a `.d.ts` (or any module), `declare module 'lib'` and re-open the interface you want to extend, relying on interface declaration merging to add fields. For globals like `Window` or Node's `process.env`, use `declare global { interface Window { ... } }` from inside a module. The file must be a module (have an import/export) for `declare global` to work, and you can only patch existing named declarations, not add new top-level exports.
fs: `declare module 'lib'` re-opens an interface to merge in fields; `declare global` patches Window/process from a module.
fj: Casting to `any` to set the custom field instead of augmenting, losing type safety everywhere it is read.

followups.q: Why must the augmentation file contain an import or export to work?
followups.a: Without a top-level `import`/`export`, the file is treated as a global script, so `declare module 'name'` is interpreted as ambient module declaration (declaring a new module) rather than augmenting an existing one. Adding `import 'express'` makes the file a module, so `declare module` correctly merges into the real package's types.

followups.q: How do you augment a global like `process.env` with typed keys?
followups.a: From inside a module, write `declare global { namespace NodeJS { interface ProcessEnv { DATABASE_URL: string } } }`. This merges into Node's existing `ProcessEnv` interface so `process.env.DATABASE_URL` is typed. Note env values are still strings and could be undefined at runtime, so the annotation is a convenience, not a guarantee.

followups.q: What goes wrong if the augmented field is required rather than optional?
followups.a: If you declare `userId: string` (required) but middleware has not set it yet, every `req` is typed as having it even before assignment, so reads compile but crash or read `undefined` at runtime. Optional (`userId?: string`) honestly models that the field may be absent until populated, forcing callers to handle the unset case.

q: What is a `.d.ts` declaration file, and when do you write one by hand?
a: A `.d.ts` file contains only ambient type declarations and no executable code; the compiler emits one per module when `declaration` is on so consumers get types without source. You write one by hand to describe an untyped JavaScript dependency, to declare global variables or non-code imports (e.g. `declare module '*.svg'`), or to ship types for a library. Inside, `declare` introduces ambient declarations that assert something exists at runtime without defining it. They participate in declaration merging and module augmentation like normal type declarations.
fs: `.d.ts` files hold ambient, code-free declarations: generated for published types or hand-written to type untyped JS/assets.
fj: Putting runtime code in a `.d.ts`, or thinking `declare` creates a value rather than just asserting its type.

followups.q: Why does `declare const __VERSION__` compile even though nothing assigns it?
followups.a: `declare` is an ambient assertion: it tells the compiler 'trust me, this exists at runtime' without emitting any code or requiring an initializer. It is your responsibility (often via a bundler's define plugin) to actually provide the value. If you are wrong, the reference is `undefined` at runtime with no compile error.

followups.q: How does a `declare module '*.svg'` wildcard differ from augmenting a named module?
followups.a: A wildcard ambient module declaration assigns a type to any import matching the glob (all `.svg` imports), which is how you type non-code assets handled by a bundler loader. Augmenting a named module re-opens an existing package's declarations to add members. The wildcard creates types for things that have no real `.d.ts` at all.

followups.q: When should you write a hand `.d.ts` versus install a `@types/...` package?
followups.a: Prefer the community `@types/<pkg>` from DefinitelyTyped when it exists, as it is maintained and complete. Hand-write a `.d.ts` only when no types exist, the types are wrong/incomplete and you need a local stub, or for project-specific globals and asset modules that no published package covers.

q: How do you write a generic class, and what does the type parameter scope to?
a: A class type parameter is declared after the class name (`class Box<T>`) and is in scope across instance members and the constructor, letting fields and methods share the same `T`. It is bound per instance, so `new Box<number>()` fixes `T` for that object. Note that `static` members cannot reference the class type parameter, because statics are shared across all instantiations and have no single `T`. Generic classes are the right tool for typed containers, stacks, caches, and result wrappers.
fs: Class `<T>` scopes to instance members and constructor, bound per instance; static members cannot use it.
fj: Trying to reference the class type parameter from a `static` method, which is a compile error.

followups.q: Why exactly can't a `static` member use the class type parameter `T`?
followups.a: Static members live on the class constructor itself, which is one shared object across all instantiations, so there is no single `T` they could bind to: `Stack<number>` and `Stack<string>` share the same statics. If a static needs to be generic, give the static method its own type parameter independent of the class's `T`.

followups.q: How do generic classes behave with respect to structural compatibility on their type parameter?
followups.a: Compatibility is judged on the resulting structural shapes, and variance follows how `T` is used. In output positions (return types) it is covariant-ish; in input positions (method params) the usual bivariant method behavior applies by default. So `Stack<Dog>` is assignable where `Stack<Animal>` is expected only as far as the member shapes allow.

followups.q: When should you prefer a generic function over a generic class for a typed container operation?
followups.a: Use a generic class when you need to hold state across calls with a fixed `T` (a stack, cache, or builder). Use a generic function when the operation is stateless and you just want to relate input and output types per call. Reaching for a class when a function suffices adds instantiation ceremony without benefit.

q: How do you correctly type an async function and the values it awaits?
a: An `async` function always returns a `Promise`, so an explicit annotation must be `Promise<T>` where `T` is the resolved value; returning a bare `T` is a mistake the compiler will flag. Inside, `await p` unwraps a `Promise<T>` to `T`, and the resolved type can be derived with `Awaited<ReturnType<typeof fn>>` rather than hand-written. Rejection is untyped in TS (no checked exceptions), so the resolved type never includes the error; model recoverable failure in the resolved type itself if callers must handle it.
fs: `async` functions return `Promise<T>`; `await` unwraps to `T`, and rejection types are not tracked by the signature.
fj: Annotating an async function's return as the resolved value `T` instead of `Promise<T>`.

lines: const u = await load() // u: User

followups.q: Why is `return r.json() as Promise<User>` a risky assertion, and what is safer?
followups.a: `r.json()` resolves to `any`, and the `as Promise<User>` is an unchecked assertion that does no runtime validation, so malformed responses are typed as valid `User` and crash later. Safer is to type the parse result as `unknown` and run a runtime validator (zod/valibot) that produces a genuinely-checked `User` before returning.

followups.q: What is the consequence of forgetting `await` on a promise inside an async function?
followups.a: You get a `Promise<T>` where you expected `T`, so property access or arithmetic on it is wrong, and errors from the promise become unhandled rejections rather than being caught by surrounding `try/catch`. The `@typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises` rule and `Promise<...>` showing up in inferred types are how you catch the omission.

followups.q: Why does returning a non-promise value from an async function still type as `Promise<T>`?
followups.a: The `async` keyword wraps the return in a resolved promise automatically, so `async () => 5` has type `() => Promise<number>`. Likewise returning a `Promise<T>` is flattened to `Promise<T>`, not `Promise<Promise<T>>`. That is why annotating the resolved value directly is an error: the actual return type is always the promise wrapper.

q: Why is `==` discouraged over `===` in TypeScript, and does the type checker save you here?
a: `==` performs type coercion with surprising rules (`'' == 0`, `null == undefined`, `[] == false` are all true), so it can pass when values are semantically different. `===` compares without coercion and is predictable, which is why lint configs enforce it. TypeScript narrows types but does not change JavaScript's runtime equality semantics, so `==` still coerces at runtime; the one common exception is `x == null`, which conveniently matches both `null` and `undefined`. Prefer `===` and explicit null/undefined checks.
fs: `==` coerces at runtime with surprising truthiness; TS does not fix that, so prefer `===` (except the `== null` idiom).
fj: Assuming TypeScript makes `==` safe, or that the type checker rejects coercing comparisons by default.

followups.q: Does `===` between two disjoint types ever get flagged by the compiler?
followups.a: Yes: comparing values whose types have no overlap (e.g. a `string` `===` a `number` literal type) triggers TS error 2367, 'This comparison appears to be unintentional.' It is a narrow safety net for clearly-impossible comparisons, but it does nothing about `==` coercion semantics, which remain a runtime concern.

followups.q: Why is `x == null` the one sanctioned use of loose equality?
followups.a: Because `==` coerces only `null` and `undefined` to be equal to each other (and to nothing else), `x == null` is a concise, correct check for 'null or undefined' that `=== null || === undefined` expresses more verbosely. Most lint configs that ban `==` carve out this exact idiom.

followups.q: How does `Object.is` differ from `===` for edge cases?
followups.a: `Object.is` is like `===` except it treats `NaN` as equal to `NaN` (whereas `NaN === NaN` is false) and distinguishes `+0` from `-0` (whereas `+0 === -0` is true). Use it when those specific edge cases matter, such as in memoization or equality utilities; for everyday comparisons `===` is sufficient.

q: How does `any` silently leak through a codebase, and how do you contain it?
a: `any` is contagious: any expression touching an `any` becomes `any`, so a single untyped import, `JSON.parse`, or `as any` cast can erase checking across a whole call chain without an error. Contain it by typing boundaries as `unknown` and narrowing, banning implicit `any` with `noImplicitAny`, and surfacing accidental `any` with the lint rule `no-unsafe-*` family or `noImplicitAny`. Treat every `as any` and untyped dependency as a hole that needs an explicit guard or a `.d.ts`, not a convenience.
fs: `any` is contagious and spreads silently; contain it with `unknown` boundaries, `noImplicitAny`, and no-unsafe lint rules.
fj: Sprinkling `as any` to silence one error, unaware it disables checking on everything downstream.

lines: const n: number = id   // no error!
// fix: type as unknown, then narrow

followups.q: Which `@typescript-eslint` rules specifically catch leaked `any` that the compiler allows?
followups.a: The `no-unsafe-*` family: `no-unsafe-assignment`, `no-unsafe-member-access`, `no-unsafe-call`, `no-unsafe-return`, and `no-unsafe-argument`. These are type-aware lint rules that flag operations on `any` values that `tsc` itself permits, plus `no-explicit-any` to forbid writing `any` outright. They are how teams enforce the `unknown`-at-boundaries discipline.

followups.q: Why does `noImplicitAny` not catch an explicit `as any` or a value typed `any` from a library?
followups.a: `noImplicitAny` only errors on implicitly-inferred `any` (untyped parameters, untyped variables). An explicit `as any` or an `any` coming from a third-party `.d.ts` is deliberate `any`, which the compiler honors silently. Containing those requires the lint rules and disciplined `unknown` boundaries, not just `noImplicitAny`.

followups.q: How does `any` differ from `unknown` in how it propagates through member access?
followups.a: Accessing a property of `any` yields `any`, so the contagion continues; accessing a property of `unknown` is a compile error until you narrow. That single difference is why `unknown` quarantines untyped data at the boundary while `any` lets it spread unchecked through every subsequent expression.

q: What do the `strict` family flags enable, and which ones bite most often?
a: `strict: true` switches on a bundle of flags together: `strictNullChecks`, `noImplicitAny`, `strictFunctionTypes`, `strictBindCallApply`, `strictPropertyInitialization`, `useUnknownInCatchVariables`, `alwaysStrict`, and `noImplicitThis`. The two that surface the most bugs are `strictNullChecks` (null/undefined become explicit) and `noImplicitAny` (untyped parameters error instead of silently becoming `any`).
